title: The Chokepoint That Never Was
subhead: The Hormuz crisis revealed not Iranian leverage but the fragility of an energy architecture built on the assumption that chokepoints would never be weaponized. Now every player is scrambling to build alternative routes while the Gulf's petrostates watch their bargaining power evaporate.

sections.heading: Hormuz: The Leverage That Wasn't
sections.body:
President Trump's announcement that the US and Iran are "closing in" on a memorandum to end the war — with the Strait of Hormuz reopening as a key term — marks the end of a three-month experiment in chokepoint warfare. The result: Iran discovered that closing Hormuz hurt Iran more than it hurt its adversaries. The closure didn't create a global energy crisis; it accelerated the construction of one.

Oil prices fell nearly 10% on news of the potential deal, with Brent dropping to $96.77 per barrel — a two-week low. The market reaction tells the real story: the structural fear premium that drove prices to $120 during peak conflict was never about supply disruption. It was about the revelation that the post-Cold War energy security architecture was built on an assumption that no state would dare weaponize Hormuz. That assumption is now dead. Every importer from India to Japan is building alternatives, and that infrastructure will outlast any peace deal.

Trump paused 'Project Freedom' — the US naval escort mission through Hormuz — after less than 48 hours, citing progress toward a comprehensive agreement. The speed of the pivot from military convoy to peace memorandum reveals the actual balance of leverage: Washington concluded that Iran wanted an exit more than it wanted to maintain the chokepoint as a permanent lever. The war's endgame is being shaped not by Iranian bargaining power but by Iranian exhaustion.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for importers is not celebrating Hormuz's reopening but accelerating the infrastructure that made Iran's leverage limited in the first place. The trap for Gulf exporters is believing that a reopened Hormuz restores the old order — it doesn't. The architecture of evasion is now permanent.

sections.heading: Norway's Gas Gambit: Europe's Insurance Premium
sections.body:
Oslo approved the reopening of three North Sea gas fields closed since 1998, committing 19 billion kroner (£1.5bn) to restart production by end of 2028 with operations running until 2048. The gas will flow via pipeline to Germany; light oil heads to the UK. Norway also opened 70 new exploration areas in the North Sea, Barents Sea, and Norwegian Sea — some closer to the coastline than ever before.

This is not about current supply shortages; it's about the permanent recognition that Russian gas is gone and Gulf gas is unreliable. The IEA's chief has described the current geoeconomic crisis as more severe than the combined shocks of 1973, 1979, and 2022. Norway's decision to extend fossil fuel production two decades beyond previous plans reflects a hard calculation: Europe would rather pay the domestic political cost of climate backsliding than remain exposed to energy coercion from either Russia or the Middle East.

Equinor reported record output and a three-year profit high driven by Iran war price spikes, but also noted a surge in interest from Asia-Pacific customers — including Australia — seeking alternatives to Gulf petroleum and LNG. The Norway model is becoming the template: produce more, export further, accept that energy security now means geographic diversification at any cost.
sections.whyItMatters: The structural shift is European acceptance of higher domestic carbon emissions as the price of strategic autonomy. Norway's decision to reopen fields near the shoreline — risking environmental catastrophe for energy independence — reveals how the threat calculus has changed. Energy security is no longer about price; it's about route control.

sections.heading: OPEC's Fracture: The UAE Walks Away
sections.body:
The UAE's withdrawal from OPEC, effective May 2026, is the most significant structural break in the cartel since its founding. The fracture with Saudi Arabia that began over production quotas has metastasized into a fundamental split over vision: Riyadh wants controlled supply to maintain prices; Abu Dhabi wants maximum production to capture market share before the transition erodes demand.

The OPEC architecture was designed to give Saudi Arabia leverage over global prices through coordinated supply management. That architecture now faces an existential test. If the UAE pumps at full capacity — potentially reaching 5 million barrels daily by 2027 — Saudi Arabia faces a binary choice: pump alongside Abu Dhabi and crash prices, or cut production and watch market share flow to its former ally.

The rift extends beyond economics. The two Gulf powers backed opposing sides in regional conflicts, pursued competing visions for the Middle East, and now run rival strategies for the post-oil era. Dubai's status as the region's financial hub is under direct threat from Saudi Vision 2030's transformation of Riyadh into a business center. OPEC's collapse would remove the primary tool Gulf states used to translate oil reserves into geopolitical leverage.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap for Saudi Arabia is believing that OPEC's mechanisms can survive the exit of its second-most important producer. The decisive move for Riyadh is either accommodation — accepting UAE production outside quota limits — or confrontation, risking a race to the bottom that benefits importers and accelerates the transition timeline.

sections.heading: Cobalt's Collapse: The Mineral Trap
sections.body:
The Democratic Republic of Congo, which produces 70% of global cobalt supply, imposed strict export quotas of 96,600 tonnes annually for 2026 and 2027 — replacing a 2025 export ban. The move is designed to stabilize prices after cobalt crashed from $77,000 per metric ton post-COVID to a nine-year low of $22,000 in 2025. But the quota regime masks a deeper shift: DRC miners are pivoting to copper.

Cobalt is primarily a copper byproduct — 99% of global cobalt comes from copper or nickel operations. Glencore has adopted a 'copper-first' strategy, prioritizing the metal where demand from AI data centers, electrification, and EVs is surging while supply tightens. The DRC government wants to incentivize domestic processing, but lithium sulphate exports still require further refining into battery-grade materials offshore. The value chain remains captured by foreign processors.

The OECD projects that global lithium supply will peak around 2030 and then decline, even as demand rises nearly six-fold by 2040. Zimbabwe's share of lithium production is expected to fall from 9.3% to 6% by 2040 despite introducing raw lithium export bans. Africa's mineral leverage is eroding not because of resource depletion but because the continent remains locked into exporting raw materials while China controls the refining and processing infrastructure that captures the value.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap for mineral-rich states is believing export restrictions capture value. The decisive move requires building processing capacity — but that competes directly with China's existing infrastructure. Without downstream investment, export bans simply redirect revenue to other producers or black markets.

sections.heading: India's Energy Calculus: Import Dependence as Leverage
sections.body:
India imports over 85% of its crude oil needs, with roughly 45% of imports transiting through Hormuz. The IEA projects India's consumption reaching 5.99 million barrels per day in 2026, making it the world's third-largest oil consumer and strategically valuable to exporters in a weakening market. OPEC forecasts global demand contraction of 80 kb/d in 2026, while India's demand continues rising.

India's vulnerability is real, but so is its leverage. In a market where OECD demand is falling and non-OECD demand is concentrated in a handful of large importers, India's consumption growth becomes a structural asset. The country has handled repeated shocks better than many comparable importers, partly by accepting higher costs for diversification. China has locked in 25 million metric tons of LNG annually through contracts; South Korea secured 273 million barrels of crude from non-Hormuz routes; Japan stockpiled 470 million barrels, equivalent to 254 days of consumption. India is building similar buffers.

The IEA's characterization of the current crisis as worse than 1973, 1979, and 2022 combined reflects not just supply disruption but the end of the old energy order's predictability. India's growth is projected to slow from 7.4% in FY26 to 6.5% in FY27, with inflation rising from 2.3% to 4.4% due to energy supply chain disruptions. The price of resilience is paid in domestic economic costs.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for India is translating import dependence into buyer leverage in a market where exporters need Indian demand more than India needs any single exporter. The trap is assuming diversification is sufficient without building the domestic processing capacity that China has used to capture the mineral value chain.

sections.heading: The G7's Mineral Dead End
sections.body:
G7 trade ministers meeting in Paris discussed creating a permanent secretariat to coordinate critical mineral supplies beyond the bloc's rotating presidencies. France, holding the G7 presidency, called an online meeting to discuss 'breaking China's stranglehold' on rare earths and other critical materials. The US and EU agreed last month to deepen coordination on critical minerals.

Chinese analysts at the Global Times correctly identified the flaw: repeatedly framing China as the problem leaves Western supply chain initiatives trapped in a deadlock. Building independent supply chains without China is 'extremely difficult' because the industry involves not just mining but refining, processing, and downstream manufacturing — areas where China has built dominant positions.

The OECD's Africa Regional Note acknowledges that no African country will contribute significantly to global lithium refining despite having mineral resources. The G7 approach assumes that de-risking from China is possible through alternative sourcing in Africa and elsewhere, but those resources still require processing infrastructure that China already possesses. Western initiatives face 'internal rifts' over whose interests come first, making unified road maps 'unsustainable' according to Chinese analysts.
sections.whyItMatters: The trap is believing that political coordination can substitute for industrial capacity. The decisive move would require building complete supply chains from mining to manufacturing — a decades-long, capital-intensive project that no G7 member wants to fund alone. Until then, de-risking remains rhetoric.

sections.heading: Türkiye's Energy Corridor Moment
sections.body:
At the European Political Community summit in Yerevan, Türkiye advanced the Middle Corridor and energy route discussions while reshaping ties with Europe beyond the stalled EU accession process. The Iran war has highlighted Türkiye's geographic position as both vulnerability and opportunity: energy routes that bypass Hormuz must transit Turkish territory or Turkish-controlled waterways to reach European markets.

Türkiye's energy leverage depends on being the alternative route, not the destination. As Gulf energy exports face chokepoint risk, the Middle Corridor through Türkiye becomes critical infrastructure for any Central Asian or Middle Eastern producer seeking reliable access to European buyers.

Russia's LNG exports rose 8.5% in early 2026 despite sanctions, with Europe increasing imports while publicly restricting Russian energy. Norway's decision to expand North Sea production reflects European desire for non-Russian, non-Gulf gas — but Norwegian pipeline capacity is limited. Any additional gas reaching Europe from the east must pass through Turkish Stream or other Turkish-controlled infrastructure.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's decisive move is positioning itself as the indispensable transit state for any energy not sourced from Norway or North Africa. The trap is overplaying the hand — if peace in Hormuz holds and Gulf flows resume, Türkiye's leverage diminishes to its pre-conflict baseline.

bottomLine: The Hormuz crisis was a stress test that revealed the structural weaknesses of the post-Cold War energy order. Iran discovered that chokepoint leverage has limits when alternatives exist and when closure hurts the blocker more than the blocked. Gulf exporters discovered that OPEC discipline cannot survive the exit of key producers pursuing divergent strategies. Mineral-rich states discovered that export restrictions cannot capture value without downstream processing capacity. And the G7 discovered that de-risking from China requires building what China already spent decades constructing. The energy map is being redrawn, but not by Western initiative. It is being reshaped by the recognition that security now means resilience, diversification, and domestic capacity — not simply access to global markets at the lowest price.
sources: Reuters, CNN, The Economist, The Guardian, OilPrice.com, Global Times (China), OECD Regional Note on Critical Minerals in Africa, Zimbabwe Situation, The Hindu, Washington Post, New York Times, Al Jazeera, IEA, OPEC
